subject: ap-csp
theme: The internet is a decentralized, packet-switched network that achieves fault tolerance through redundancy, and parallel/distributed computing can dramatically increase processing efficiency.
core_concepts: The internet is decentralized and packet-switched — data is broken into packets that travel independently and may take different routes to the destination.
DNS translates domain names (www.example.com) to IP addresses; IPv4 uses 32 bits (~4 billion addresses); IPv6 uses 128 bits (vastly more addresses).
TCP is reliable (acknowledges, retransmits lost packets); UDP is faster but unreliable (no guaranteed delivery) — used for real-time applications.
Fault tolerance is achieved through redundancy — multiple paths and duplicate servers mean the network continues functioning when components fail.
Parallel computing divides a task among multiple processors running simultaneously; sequential computing processes steps one at a time.
Distributed computing spreads work across multiple networked machines — tasks with sequential dependencies limit the speedup achievable.
Bandwidth is the maximum data transfer rate — insufficient bandwidth causes buffering or reduced quality in streaming applications.
exam_tip: The CB frequently tests fault tolerance (how redundancy provides reliability), parallel vs. sequential computing speedup calculations, and the specific roles of DNS, TCP, and UDP.
